summary: Japan's approach is evaluation-led and is now firming into a dated plan. CRYPTREC, the national cryptographic evaluation body, publishes post-quantum cryptography guidelines and has added a post-quantum list as Table 2 of the CRYPTREC cipher list. A cross-ministerial liaison council run from the Cabinet Secretariat's National Cyber Unit set a target of completing the migration of government agencies to post-quantum cryptography by around 2035, with a roadmap to be drawn up in the 2026 financial year, and METI has set the same approximate 2035 deadline for industry with adjustments according to how sensitive the information is. The Digital Agency carries the migration among its trust-policy priorities, the Financial Services Agency has issued recommendations for deposit-taking financial institutions, and the Bank of Japan, MIC and NICT support the work through guidance, evaluation and research.
migrationTimeline:
2026 | Government post-quantum migration roadmap to be drawn up (FY2026)
2035 | Government and, as a rule, industry migration to post-quantum cryptography to be completed, adjusted by information criticality
govActors:
CRYPTREC | National cryptographic technology evaluation body, publishes PQC guidelines and maintains the PQC cipher list
Cabinet Secretariat | Runs the cross-ministerial PQC liaison council through its National Cyber Unit and set the around-2035 government migration target
Digital Agency | Carries PQC migration in its trust-policy priorities and commissioned a study on migration
METI | Industrial cybersecurity lead, set the around-2035 PQC migration deadline for industry
FSA | Financial Services Agency, issued recommendations for deposit-taking financial institutions migrating to PQC
BOJ | Bank of Japan, explains PQC and how financial institutions should respond
MIC | Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, evaluates and funds cryptographic R&D toward PQC
NICT | National research institute supporting PQC R&D and reporting implementation and deployment status
mainRegulation:
Common Standards for Cybersecurity Measures for Government Agencies (government cryptography-use rules) | national | binding-by-market-access
CRYPTREC Post-Quantum Cryptography list (Table 2 of the CRYPTREC cipher list) | national | guidance
CRYPTREC Cryptographic Technology Guideline on Post-Quantum Cryptography (FY2024 edition) | national | guidance
Cybersecurity Strategy (government PQC migration measures, December 2025) | national | soft-law
Liaison Council on the Use of Post-Quantum Cryptography in Government Agencies (interim summary, around-2035 target) | national | soft-law
FSA Study Group report on PQC response for deposit-taking financial institutions | national | guidance
obligation: Government agencies procuring information systems must, under the Common Standards set by the Cybersecurity Strategic Headquarters, specify ciphers from the CRYPTREC Ciphers List in their procurement requirements, which makes the list binding by market access for government suppliers. This currently governs cryptography generally rather than mandating post-quantum algorithms; CRYPTREC has evaluated and is adding the NIST post-quantum algorithms (ML-KEM and others), and its post-quantum guideline remains advisory. There is no general post-quantum obligation on the private sector.
classificationBasis:
CRYPTREC guidelines + FY2024 survey: ingested CRYPTREC rows
FIPS 203/204/205: CRYPTREC evaluation-committee report (ingested)
Cross-ministerial council: Cabinet Secretariat row
Digital Agency priorities: FY2026 budget row
Mixed process: CRYPTREC national evaluation framework processing NIST standards - REVIEW
No timeline/target set: 2035 figure appears only in vendor sources, excluded per iron rule
